history.label: 16th century
history.text: Havana is the prevailing name for the city found in English-language dictionaries. 16th century Beginnings Diego Velázquez originally founded Havana in 1514, on the opposite coast, 69 km (43 mi) south of its current.
Between 1514 and 1519, the Spanish established the first settlements on the northern coast of Cuba near present-day Havana.
The town that became Havana originated adjacent to what was then called Puerto de Carenas (literally, 'Careening Port'), in 1519.

history.text: In the meantime, efforts to build or improve the defensive infrastructure of the city continued. 17th century Havana expanded greatly during the 17th century.

history.label: 20th century
history.text: On 12 August 1898, Spain relinquished sovereignty over Cuba, thus ending Spanish presence in the Americas. 20th century Republican era Havana became the capital of the newly independent Republic of Cuba in 1902.

history.text: The first cities founded on the island served as little more than bases for the conquista of other lands. Attacks As Havana began as a trading port in the Caribbean, it suffered regular attacks.
